title: GitHub Actions & Docker Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on CI pipelines, tagging Docker images, and GitHub contexts.

questions.question: What happens on the GitHub Actions servers when a workflow is triggered?

options.text: It executes the commands strictly on the developer's laptop.
It spins up a temporary virtual machine or container (a runner), clones the code, and executes the defined steps.
It instantly deploys the code directly to AWS via SSH.
It compiles the source code into binary.

questions.question: Why is it considered a bad practice to rely solely on the 'latest' Docker image tag for production deployments?

options.text: Because 'latest' is a mutable tag; if a pod restarts, it might pull an unverified newer build unexpectedly.
Because DockerHub charges extra fees for hosting images explicitly labeled 'latest'.
Because Kubernetes cannot parse the word 'latest'.
Because 'latest' is reserved strictly for local development.

questions.question: What is the recommended method for tagging Docker images built via CI/CD for enterprise applications?

options.text: Using a simple incrementing integer (+1, +2).
Tagging them dynamically with the developer's first name.
Tagging them with an immutable, unique identifier like the short Git Commit SHA (${{ github.sha }}).
Leaving the tag entirely blank.

questions.question: In a GitHub Actions YAML file, what does 'uses: actions/checkout@v3' do?

options.text: It clones the Git repository into the temporary runner so the subsequent commands can access the code.
It creates a new Git branch.
It checks out (purchases) a new EC2 instance from AWS.
It commits any changes made during the test run back to the repository.

questions.question: What is the primary technical benefit of utilizing Docker layer caching (actions/cache) in a GitHub Actions workflow?

options.text: It automatically tests the code for syntax errors.
It prevents unauthorized users from executing deployments.
It drastically reduces build execution times by reusing unchanged Docker layers (like downloaded npm dependencies) from previous runs.
It creates a backup copy of the image on Amazon S3.
